data.title: DoorDash: Time To Lock In Gains On Expansive M&A Deals And Potential Deceleration
data.description:
The stock market has reached new intraday highs, and with it, valuation multiples are appearing more stretched than ever. The core question that is important for investors to consider is: what can derail this rally, and what's the best way to protect our portfolios from downside?

For me, I've been locking in gains on a lot of long positions that, I think, have gotten too stretched, and DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) is one of them. The delivery app company has seen its share price soar more than 40% this year, and the truth is that the rise has been accompanied by very strong execution in the wake of a choppy macro. And yet, when I assess the position freshly at current levels, I find less appeal in the stock and its ability to keep rallying.

I last wrote a buy opinion on DoorDash in February, when the stock was still trading near $190 per share. Since then, my position has appreciated another ~20%.

At the time, I had cheered DoorDash for its wider growth premiums versus UberEats (UBER), as well as the company's consistent pace of adjusted EBITDA expansion. But while these factors are still very much true, I am concerned about A) the recent slew of deals the company has announced, and B) the potential for orders to drop off sharply amid the tougher macro and weaker consumer spending (a macroeconomic reality that the broader stock market, in rallying back to all-time highs, appears to be ignoring).

As such, I'm downgrading my rating on DoorDash to neutral and recommending investors to lock in gains here. Let's now dig into the reasons why.

Grandiose M&A deals will consume all of DoorDash's current balance sheet

DoorDash recently announced two marquee M&A deals: in May, the company announced its intention to buy out Deliveroo, a direct peer in London, for £2.4 billion (or roughly $3.29 billion in U.S. dollar terms, and at a 44% premium to Deliveroo's trading price before the deal was announced). Also in May, the company spent $1.2 billion to acquire SevenRooms, which is a booking platform for restaurants and hotels with various operational software tools for these industries as well.

More details on the acquisition are in the snapshot below, taken from DoorDash's recent Q1 shareholder letter:

I have mixed feelings on these deals, needless to say. I do view the Deliveroo acquisition as a natural extension of DoorDash's platform. We note that DoorDash was already the largest delivery service provider in the U.S. (in Q1, the company's $23.1 billion of marketplace GOV actually topped Uber's $20.4 billion in the same quarter, in the delivery segment only and excluding rideshare), and for DoorDash to take its formula and accelerate its overseas expansion into the UK first makes sense.

The SevenRooms deal, however, is more contentious for me. DoorDash is primarily a consumer-oriented business: whereas SevenRooms is enterprise-facing, targeting hotels and restaurants (including giant chains of each of these). I don't view SevenRoom's booking platform as being able to compete with much larger names like OpenTable and Resy, while its software competes against companies like Toast (TOST) that have made significant inroads into becoming the all-in-one operational system for restaurants.

Then there's the sheer size of the deals involved. Across these two all-cash transactions, DoorDash is spending ~$4.5 billion in U.S. dollar terms. Meanwhile, the company's most recent balance sheet shows only $6.37 billion in cash, restricted cash, and investments - meaning that DoorDash is plowing the majority portion of its resources into these two mega-deals.

Deceleration may be starting to kick in

DoorDash is paying hefty premiums to expand its empire at the exact time that its business may be slowing down, too. Take a look at the Q1 trends below:

Marketplace GOV (gross order volume) growth slowed to 20% y/y in Q1, while revenue of $3.03 billion slowed to 21% y/y growth, a four-point deceleration relative to 25% y/y growth in Q4. We note that DoorDash's revenue no longer sits at a higher growth rate than Uber, which grew 22% y/y in constant-currency terms in Q4 (DoorDash, which is primarily a U.S. business, has virtually equivalent constant-currency and as-reported growth rates, at least until the Deliveroo deal closes and the company intakes a much greater percentage of pounds in its revenue base). This compares to DoorDash growing 25% y/y in Q4 to Uber's 20% y/y growth.

But even regardless of the competition between the two main delivery giants, the entire macroeconomy may be spelling trouble for the restaurant industry. One metric that I keep pointing to is the fact that Chipotle (CMG), which is the undisputed bellwether of the fast-casual restaurant industry, saw its same-restaurant sales growth slow to a -0.4% y/y decline in Q1, a 580bps deceleration versus 5.4% growth in Q4:

Consumers are finally beginning to push back against price increases, and it's not just Chipotle feeling the heat: from giants like McDonalds (MCD) to emerging chains like Sweetgreen (SG), most restaurant companies reported weaker same-restaurant sales in Q1, and that trend is likely to worsen in Q2 and beyond as macroeconomic impacts are more deeply felt.

Despite DoorDash's orientation as a tech platform, it is still ultimately dependent on consumers' restaurant ordering trends: and with deceleration set to kick in, I fear that DoorDash's valuation premiums may compress.

Valuation and key takeaways

To me, the main factor that keeps me sidelined and incentivized to lock in gains is the fact that DoorDash has soared to rich valuations. As shown in the chart below, DoorDash's valuation multiples have gradually been gliding upward, to hit 35x forward adjusted EBITDA and 7.5x forward revenue:

These multiples may be more defendable when the company is still achieving accelerating growth, but with broader restaurant trends set to cast a pall on DoorDash's growth premium, I don't think these valuation multiples are sustainable: especially when the company is set to spend the lion's share of its balance sheet for two untested acquisitions.

Long term, I still think DoorDash will be one of the dominant delivery platforms globally, which prevents me from being a complete bear on the stock: but at the same time, I do think DoorDash is primed for a near-term correction and investors will have a chance to buy back in at a more favorable price.

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Labor Market Getting Weaker?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nonfarm payrolls hit on Thursday due to the July 4 holiday. (0:17) Constellation Brands reports: bull vs. bear. (1:06) Senate GOP clears bill hurdle. (2:12)

The following is an abridged transcript:

It’s jobs week again and traders get the numbers early due to the market holiday for Independence Day.

The markets are closed Friday July 4th and will close at 1 p.m. on Thursday, the same day payrolls hit before the bell.

Economists expect that June nonfarm payrolls rose by 120,000, with the unemployment rate staying at 4.2% and average hourly earnings up 0.3%.

Wells Fargo Economists say: "The labor market is losing altitude. Although payrolls came in slightly better than expected in May, significant downward revisions to March and April unveiled a softer pace of expansion."

"Looking through the household survey's monthly noise, the softening in labor demand will likely keep the unemployment rate inching higher on trend," they added. "The labor market's gradual loosening has already eased the pressure on employers to hike wages."

Also on the economic calendar, the ISM indexes for manufacturing and services are due, as well as factory orders and construction spending.

The earnings calendar remains light, but drinks company Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) is due to report on Tuesday.

EPS is expected to decline to $3.29 on a revenue of $2.56 billion. As the company continues to face growth stagnation and tariff headwinds, analysts expect outlook to be weak, with the wine and spirits segment struggling with double-digit revenue declines.

In the bear camp, SA analyst Luca Socci says valuation “appears stretched, with an intrinsic value below the current price and negative momentum increasing downside risk, especially before earnings.”

But SA analyst Jim Sloan is more optimistic, noting that Berkshire Hathaway bought a position in STZ in Q4 2024 and doubled it in Q1 2025, “seeing a solid value investment cheap because of temporary risks.”

Buffett or his lieutenants “considered it good value when buying it up to 10-15% down from the top, then saw nothing in the macro challenges to make (them) change (their) mind, and instead jumped on a chance to buy 114% more at prices 30% or so lower,”

Also on the earnings calendar:

Progress Software (PRGS) reports on Monday.

Joining Constellation Brands are MSC Industrial Direct (MSM) and The Greenbrier Companies (GBX).

UniFirst (UNF) weighs in on Wednesday.

In the news this weekend

Senate Republicans narrowly cleared a key hurdle late Saturday to keep President Trump’s ambitious tax and spending package alive, though the razor-thin vote exposed the political headaches that lie ahead for GOP leaders.

In a 51-49 vote, most Republicans backed the bill’s advancement, while Democrats uniformly opposed it. Senators Rand Paul (Republican, Kentucky) and Thom Tillis (Republican, North Carolina) broke with their party, voting no.

The bill runs nearly 1,000 pages and remains subject to revisions. Senate leaders hope to pass the legislation by Monday, aligning with Trump’s July 4 target to finalize the package.

And the FT reports that executives and board members at Nvidia (NVDA) sold more than $1 billion of company shares in the past year. A significant portion of those sales occurring in recent weeks as investor excitement over artificial intelligence drives the stock to record highs.

Roughly $500 million in insider sales took place this month alone as Nvidia’s market valuation soared, cementing its position as the world’s most valuable company. The surge comes despite recent geopolitical headwinds, including escalating U.S.-China tensions and competition from Chinese AI developers, which had briefly weighed on the stock.

For income investors:

Deere (DE) goes ex-dividend on Monday, paying out on August 8.

Heico (HEI) goes ex-dividend on Tuesday, with a July 15 payout date.

Cisco (CSCO) and Progressive (PGR) go ex-dividend on Thursday. Cisco pays out on July 23 and Progressive pays out on July 11.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, the S&P 500 (SP500) is on the cusp of the seventh breakout since 1990, according to BofA.

And strategist Michael Hartnett says this breakout has the smallest “breakout stock” participation.

Within the index, 22 stocks are currently at all-time highs compared to 67 during January 2024’s breakout, 54 during August 2020’s breakout, 97 during March 2013’s breakout, 82 during May 2007’s, 66 during November 1998’s, and 51 during February 1991’s breakout.

Some of the 22 stocks include Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), IBM (IBM), Jabil (JBL), Cardinal Health (CAH), Take Two (TTWO), Philip Morris (PM), Oracle (ORCL), Amphenol (APH), Netflix (NFLX), Royal Caribbean (RCL), JPMorgan (JPM), GE Vernova (GEV), and Howmet Aerospace (HWM).

In addition, 83 stocks in the S&P 500 are within 5% of their all-time highs, and 146 are within 10%.

data.title: CrowdStrike: Effective Capital Allocation Created A Powerful Platform (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
CrowdStrike has negligible competition from the other platformization companies. Its intelligent and single agent is taking over the cybersecurity market. Quoting the 10K “we believe that the architecture of our cloud-native, single agent platform fundamentally differentiates us compared to both next-gen and legacy competitors in the security industry”. There are smaller competitors that might hurt CrowdStrike prospects, such as SentinelOne (S). Overall, I prefer to buy CrowdStrike over SentinelOne due to better capital allocation, platform and financials. CrowdStrike, for now, is showing a very high premium. Thus, I consider it a hold.

Background

CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) is one of my biggest positions in my portfolio. To keep up with the name and stock position, I want to do timely articles on the topic.

In my last article, I talked about how CrowdStrike has been executing its cloud-native lightweight agent strategy better than Palo Alto’s platformization (PANW).

Even at a large premium I prefer CrowdStrike over its biggest adversary. Valuation matters, but since I’m looking to hold companies for around 7-10 years, quality is more important than valuation.

Since my last publication, CrowdStrike has been outperforming the SP500, showing that the cybersecurity industry is critical and could outperform in bear markets.

Cybersecurity is a highly disruptive industry. Threat actors keep evolving and using the most advanced tools, such as LLMs, to uncover new vulnerabilities and powerful malwares.

New tactics are also employed. The highest growth one is free malware attacks. 79% of detections in 2024 were malware-free, up from 40% in 2019. Malware free attacks are more dangerous because they blend in with common software processes, using no malware executable programs, and inflicting a lot of damage.

CrowdStrike outperforms legacy tools in detecting free malware attacks due to its high precision lightweight agent that signals devious behavior in the environment.

Because of the fast and evolving cybersecurity landscape, we should own the most powerful and highly rated companies. In my opinion, CrowdStrike with its intelligent lightweight agent, massive data moat, strong, overall, platform and excellent capital allocation should outperform other cybersecurity companies and win most of the market in the long term.

Strategy

Despite the strong CrowdStrike status as the leading next-gen cybersecurity player it faces some competition. One company that might reduce CrowdStrike’s effective revenue is SentinelOne (S). SentinelOne uses strong AI protection, with the goal of automating most of the security operations center.

SentinelOne performed very well in the latest MITRE evaluation, with 100% protection and 88% fewer alerts than the median. Comparing the two companies, CrowdStrike has more modules providing a stronger platform, but SentinelOne is well diversified as well.

According to this source, SentinelOne has a less comprehensive offering, requiring sometimes more out-of-the-box integrations. The threat inteligence is less powerful because SentinelOne focuses on automating most of the triage. On the other hand, it leaves the client's SOC with lower visibility over what is happening compared to CrowdStrike’s offering.

The lack of direct visibility is due to SentinelOne's architecture. Additionally, the agent is heavier because it has to process the machine learning algorithms directly on the endpoint.

SentinelOne’s architecture is agent-driven and endpoint-centric. The AI-powered agent operates independently of the cloud, helping provide protection when devices are offline. This local processing enables faster response times on individual endpoints but may limit large-scale cross-environment visibility compared to CrowdStrike’s cloud-focused model. SentinelOne’s architecture supports hybrid environments, including those with legacy systems.

Link

Overall, I prefer CrowdStrike's architecture and intelligent lightweight agent that intelligently sends filtered data to the cloud. This process enables more connectivity, improving the data moat and gaining more visibility over the complete attack surface.

In the next two bold statements we dissect part of CrowdStrike’s strategy, starting with platformization, which is defending CRWD from the SentinelOne’s competitive pressure.

Consolidate customers in the platform, exchanging lower short-term revenue for long-term price increases. Falcon Flex, for me, is a very intelligent move from CrowdStrike’s management. Although it sacrifices short-term revenue, it provides a larger number of clients with more consolidation.

Due to the very high gross retention metrics, that signal high product quality, CrowdStrike is capturing more market under its brand, which could lead to timely price increases in the future.

Additionally, platformization lowers costs and improves client protection.

Our industry has to change the paradigm by shifting from fragmentation to platformization to enable the best security outcomes. In a recent study we did with IBM, platformized organizations takes 72 days less to detect and 84 days less to contain a security incident.

Link

Buy the core IP of a cyber product then quickly integrate and develop the product into a comprehensive, high-quality offering, processed by CrowdStrike’s lightweight agent. Typically, I dislike companies that are very intensive in making acquisitions. Especially in the high stakes software industry.

Acquisitions should increase tech debt. This means that by not developing the product from the start, to correctly integrate with the buying company's platform, there could be performance problems and incompatibilities. Reducing product quality.

But there are benefits to acquisitions. By acquiring, rather than building the core product, CrowdStrike is advancing more rapidly their platformization strategy. With a very powerful team of engineers and managers, I trust the company to reduce tech debt and make high quality capital allocation decisions.

Most of CrowdStrike’s highest growth products core base was bought rather than built. For example, their next-gen SIEM was catapulted by Humio’s acquisition.

Financials

In the last section I touched in SentinelOne AI capabilities. Its Purple AI SOC LLM should provide strong competition for CrowdStrike. In an environment, where LLM’s are going to start to take over the cybersecurity operations and attacks.

Here, I wish to compare both companies, understanding their relative performance and historical financial quality.

Looking at the gross margins we can detect somewhat of a problem with CrowdStrike’s performance.

YoY and over the course of the year, CRWD’s gross margins have been decreasing substantially. While SentinelOne’s increased by a significant amount. This could be because of the impact of the outage. CRWD management decided to give greater discounts, to keep customers happy and engaged with the platform.

Falcon Flex also produces lower gross margins because of high discounts to companies looking to consolidate on the Falcon platform.

Evaluating each company’s efficiency at producing FCF, we can confirm that CrowdStrike is at a much higher level than SentinelOne. But we also need to consider that S is much less mature than CrowdStrike.

One of the major factors for CrowdStrike low employed capital needs is its non-current unearned revenue. SentinelOne also boasts interesting numbers for its size, but they have been decreasing in the past year.

On the other hand, CRWD’s metric has been increasing in the past year. Flex is working and is generating a lot of cash flow, through long term contracts, that is going to be used in acquisitions and buybacks.

Analysts are more positive about CrowdStrike prospects. Even at a much higher size, CRWD is expected to grow faster than SentinelOne. In addition, CrowdStrike is expected to accelerate while SentinelOne is estimated to decelerate.

Comparing both architectures and protection results, I believe both companies should grow at a fast pace. But I prefer CrowdStrike agent architecture, and the Falcon Flex strategy, which should catapult the company faster than SentinelOne.

If we compare both companies on a FWD P/S basis, CrowdStrike looks massively overvalued. But considering CrowdStrike’s strategy, excellent execution, capital allocation, and the stronger platform advantage. In my opinion, as a very long-term holder, I see lower risks in longing CRWD, producing at the same time strong returns, than S.

Despite this, S could be as well a very strong play.

Opportunities

One of the main advantages of CrowdStrike over the other players is the usage of a very intelligent and hard to copy lightweight agent that carries multiple high-quality modules, offering protection across the board while reducing complexity and eventually producing cost savings.

Machine identities and workloads are going to explode in the long term. Because of this, there will be more need to deploy more Falcon agents to protect the enterprises. Providing a strong secular trend for CrowdStrike.

In a recent market survey, 96% of respondents plan to expand their use of AI agents in the next 12 months with 2/3 already building agents and some targeting to reach over 1 billion in production agents. […] Just as enterprises need best-in-class protection for devices, data workloads and human identities, every AI agent has the same needs, too. As an AI-first company, CrowdStrike is uniquely positioned to secure the identity, the workload, the infrastructure, the data and underlying AI models themselves. We have the platform. We have the expertise. We have a track record.

Q1 2026

Risks

As mentioned before, by acquiring the core components, instead of developing them in-house there might be problems with product quality and performance. For now, it appears that acquisitions are a must for most cybersecurity companies. So, we need to pick the ones which show the best integration of acquisitions and capital allocation.

According to CrowdStrike 10k, bad and poorly acquired acquisitions signal the end of many legacy providers that were transitioning from on-premises software to the cloud. Even including more advanced software providers that buy disparate products and badly integrate them into a fragmented platform.

Many on-premise vendors have tried to solve this problem by simply extending on-premise products to the cloud. Since their products were not purpose built to run in the cloud, traditional on-premise issues such as complex deployments, data silos, lack of integrations, limited scalability, and high maintenance costs continue to manifest. […] Some other vendors attempt to solve this problem by acquiring disparate products and stitching them together into fragmented platforms

10k

Valuation

Still, valuation is important. Even if I’m dollar cost averaging CrowdStrike, I always try to buy more when it looks less expensive.

In the next reverse DCF, where I obtain the revenue growth by considering the current price, I’m using as inputs a discount rate of 9% and a terminal growth rate of 4.5%.

To the average SP500 return, I remove 1%. CrowdStrike seems the lowest risky play in Cybersecurity, for the longer term.

For the average terminal growth rate of 3%, I add 1.5% because cybersecurity spending is very strong and should continue to grow at high paces even after a time horizon of ten years.

The TTM FCF margin is 26%. So, to be conservative, I consider a 23% FCF margin in the model.

I also account for dilution. In the calculations, I’m using a 2% dilution of share count across the years.

The model outputs a CARG growth of around 34% for the next three years. CrowdStrike is being assigned a very high premium. It’s a battle ground stock where some believe that the premium is fair while others are less enthusiastic about the valuation.

Conclusion

I believe that CrowdStrike is one of the higher quality names in the market right now. Both considering its competition, its product quality, its financials and the secular trends enabling its solutions. For the very long term, I’m much more inclined to buy CrowdStrike rather than SentinelOne, one of its major competitors. But SentinelOne might prove a strong play as well.

Given the valuation, for now, I’m assigning a hold for CrowdStrike. If it drops around 10%, I’m looking to buy more.

data.title: The Market May Be About To Get Turned Upside Down
data.description:
It is common for investors to get hung up on recency bias and to chase momentum. This is because when you frequently monitor the stock market, and you see a pattern repeating itself day after day, where a certain stock or sector continues to soar higher while another stock or sector continues to flounder, it can be easy to become conditioned to think that that pattern is going to remain in place indefinitely. Yes, there is a human behavior factor to markets, there is such a thing as irrational exuberance, and certainly, there are various momentum investing strategies that have proven to have their merits.

However, it is important to keep in mind that, on a strictly mathematical basis, the stock market is nothing more than buying and selling pieces of individual businesses. Those underlying businesses perform based on economic factors and management capital allocation decisions. Therefore, just because a stock price is rising or falling, it does not mean that the underlying business's value is falling or rising in exact tandem with it. This is where the whole value investing philosophy finds its basis, namely that based on human emotions and a tendency to get caught up in the madness of crowds, major market disconnects emerge and create valuable buying opportunities for investors.

This is what we do in that we look for opportunities where businesses or asset classes have compelling fundamentals and outlooks, yet are not popular at the moment and therefore look significantly discounted. We then buy and patiently wait for a major market reversal to take place and deliver significant upside for us. Recent examples of how we have profited from this in a major way include buying up precious metals and miners like gold (GLD), silver (SLV), Newmont Corporation (NEM) and several others, as well as midstream infrastructure (AMLP) like Energy Transfer (ET) and Plains All American Pipeline (PAA)(PAGP) in the wake of their distribution cuts in 2020. Eventually, the market realized that they were gravely mispricing these assets and went through a major reversal and completely turned itself upside down in its sentiment on these stocks.

In this article, I am going to detail once again why I think the market is about to get turned upside down.

Why The Market May Be About To Get Turned Upside Down

Right now, the environment is such that the S&P 500 (SPY) and NASDAQ (QQQ) are hitting all-time highs, fueled by the AI boom. In fact, sentiment has gotten so bullish that the market is considered to be significantly overvalued according to several leading valuation models. Meanwhile, many REITs (VNQ) and even some AI infrastructure companies have been floundering.

However, I believe that certain REITs and AI infrastructure opportunities are set to soar even as the broader market is likely going to experience a period of underperformance, thereby turning the market upside down.

While my concerns about the broader market are largely fueled by valuations, I also have concerns about the effect that further geopolitical tensions may have on the market, especially given the ongoing war in Ukraine, the potential for a revival of tensions in the Middle East between Israel and Iran, and the growing threat of Chinese aggression against Taiwan or the Philippines in Asia. Additionally, there is significant uncertainty about the status of trade relations between the United States and leading trading partners such as China, Canada, Mexico, Japan, South Korea, and the European Union.

Meanwhile, the REIT and AI infrastructure sectors are likely going to be quite defensive in nature due to their highly contracted nature and domestic U.S. focus. In the REIT sector, I especially like multifamily and timberland REITs right now. I like multifamily because it is very defensive in nature due to the fact that it provides an essential service. It is also inflation resistant in the sense that its leases tend to be shorter duration, making them quicker to adjust to spikes in inflation. The U.S. annual demand-supply ratio once again indicates that the multifamily market has moved into the undersupply cycle.

As such, I think that several blue-chip multifamily REITs that have floundered recently, such as Camden Property Trust (CPT) and Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), are once again looking like attractive investment opportunities.

There are other, even more attractive opportunities if you are willing to go into the more niche, smaller-sized multifamily space. For investors looking for blue-chips to play a broader trend, I think that MAA and CPT are solid choices.

In the timberland space, there appears to be a major public-private market disconnect, opening up an opportunity for REITs to sell non-core assets and recycle the proceeds into repurchasing shares at a steep discount to their underlying NAVs. For example, Weyerhaeuser (WY) currently trades at a 33% discount to its NAV and Rayonier (RYN) trades at a 35% discount to its NAV. Additionally, there are many higher and better uses of timberland that these REITs are pursuing to unlock additional value, whether it be housing developments, solar farms, or carbon capture leases.

Finally, in artificial intelligence infrastructure, while some companies like Vistra Corporation (VST) have seen a huge boost to their stock price from the AI boom, there are several leading infrastructure companies that seem to have gotten left behind with relatively anemic performance despite very strong growth, tailwinds, and fundamental performance. These include Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC). Between their 5-6% yields, double-digit projected AFFO per unit CAGRs for the foreseeable future that are largely being fueled by demand for electric power generation (via BEP's massive power purchase agreement with Microsoft (MSFT) and significant nuclear power tailwinds and data center developments by BIP), and BBB+ credit ratings, they appear to be highly compelling buys right now.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, while momentum investing is exciting and can be emotionally easier, value investing takes a lot of discipline and can provide extremely strong returns if you can remain disciplined and patient. We have experienced this in the past in sectors like precious metals and midstream infrastructure. Meanwhile, we think that we will be similarly rewarded for our patience in segments like multifamily real estate, timberland real estate, and certain segments of AI infrastructure. This is why these are some of our big bets at the moment at High Yield Investor.

data.title: GE Vernova: All Ready For The Energy Revolution
data.description:
Why GE Vernova?

GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) is one of the best positioned companies to benefit from the growing global demand for electricity, especially given the boom in AI-powered data centers and the need for modern, efficient networks. The large backlog provides excellent revenue visibility for the next few years, giving investors confidence in its long-term growth prospects.

However, while GEV's fundamentals are compelling and the company is improving its margins and cash flow, I believe much of this potential is already priced into the stock at current levels. The premium valuation reflects high expectations for flawless execution and continued momentum in turbine prices and new orders. In the absence of further margin expansion or exceptional new contracts, the upside potential appears limited, making patience or waiting for a better entry point the most prudent approach at this time.

I am also convinced that the company will continue to grow, and I am confident in an improvement in margins, but in an objective analysis we must put aside hopes to make room for the numbers and to date, the financial data speak clearly.

Company Overview

GE Vernova is a division of General Electric. Its mission is to decarbonize the world and make electricity more reliable, sustainable and affordable. The company focuses on the global energy transition, which is the shift from fossil fuels to cleaner energy such as highly efficient gas, renewables and advanced grid solutions.

GE Vernova operates in three main areas:

  1. Power – This division produces large-scale gas turbines, used to generate electricity in traditional power plants. The company also offers long-term maintenance services, a source of recurring revenue.
  2. Wind – This includes both onshore and offshore wind businesses. GE Vernova produces wind blades and components through LM Wind Power, one of the world's leading companies in this sector. Wind, although less profitable due to high costs, is a strategic area.
  3. Electrification – This division deals with everything needed to get electricity from the point of generation to homes and businesses. We are talking about transformers, high voltage systems (HVDC), switchgear, and grid management software. Electrification is crucial to integrating renewables and making grids more stable and safe, especially as the use of technologies such as data centers and electric vehicle charging increases.

Backlog

GE Vernova’s backlog, or the total value of contracts signed but still to be delivered, has reached over $123 billion at the beginning of 2025. A frightening number, if you think about it. This means that the company already has guaranteed orders and revenues to be realized in the coming years, giving investors visibility on future earnings.

Corporate Vision

GE Vernova states on its official website that it wants to be the technology partner that allows to reduce CO₂ emissions, building a more sustainable and equitable energy future. The company estimates that huge investments in electrical infrastructure and more efficient power plants will be needed in the coming decades to support the growing demand for electricity globally.

Financial Performance

It is good to start with an overview of the last earnings season. In the first quarter of 2025, GE Vernova showed significant growth in almost all key indicators:

  • Orders: $10.2 billion, up 8% organically compared to the same quarter last year. This is important because it indicates how many new orders are coming in: more orders mean future revenue.
  • Revenue: $8.0 billion, up 15% organically. Organic growth excludes the effect of foreign exchange or acquisitions/sales of assets, so it truly reflects the improvement of the existing business.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin: 5.7%, more than double from 2.6% in 1Q 2024. a figure that shows that the company is improving operational efficiency and is able to retain a higher share of revenues as operating profit.
  • Free cash flow (FCF): $975 million, with a conversion to net income of 369%: this means that GEV generated much more cash than what was shown by the accounting net profit, a very positive sign for financial health.

Let's open the hood and take a closer look at the various segments:

  1. Power: revenues up 16%, with EBITDA margin up to 11.5%. The increase is mainly linked to strong demand for gas turbines, improved average prices per kW and greater profitability of contracts.
  2. Electrification: even better performance, with revenues up 18% and EBITDA margin up from 4% to 11.4%. This confirms the trend of growing demand for network equipment and HVDC systems, also driven by investments in data centers.
  3. Wind: mixed signal. Revenues grew by 15%, but the EBITDA margin remained negative at -7.9%. This shows that despite the volumes, the issues related to costs and logistics of wind persist, especially in offshore.

These results clearly show that GEV is well positioned to take advantage of the growing demand for energy and electricity infrastructure, with an impressive improvement in profitability. However, the Wind segment remains a weak point that could continue to weigh on overall margins, especially if the cost dynamics in offshore and the complexity of supply chains do not stabilize. A common theme for almost all companies in this business, after all.

Let's focus on the clear improvement in free cash flow: for an industrial company, generating cash consistently is an indicator of solidity. If GEV can further improve Wind profitability, the potential for further expansion of overall margins is significant.

Price Target & Comparables

According to Bloomberg estimates, GE Vernova is now trading at around 28x EV/EBITDA on 2026 estimates, significantly higher than peers such as Eaton, Siemens Energy and Fuji Electric, which on average trade at 15–25x. This premium reflects GEV’s outstanding visibility into future revenues and its unique position in the global energy transition, but it also means the company will need to maintain excellent operating performance to justify these levels.

From the guidance and segment trends we estimate for 2026:

  • Expected 2026 revenues = USD 40.4 billion
  • Expected EBITDA margin = 11.4%
  • Estimated 2026 EBITDA = USD 40.4 × USD 0.114 = USD 4.6 billion

To calculate a conservative fair value, we apply a target multiple of 25x:

  • EV fair value = 4.6 × 25 = 115 billion USD

Add the net cash position, estimated at approximately 8 billion USD:

  • Equity fair value = 115 + 8 = 123 billion USD

Estimating 273 million diluted shares:

  • Target price = 123 billion USD / 273 million shares = 450 USD/share

I therefore believe that a target price of USD 450 already largely reflects the growth potential resulting from the current record backlog and the expected improvement in margins in the coming years. However, I consider it possible that the stock will reach higher valuations only under specific conditions:

  • a further sustainable increase in margins, especially in the Power and Electrification segments, which are already showing positive trends but which should expand well beyond the 11–12% forecast for 2026;
  • a structural improvement in the Wind segment, which to date continues to record operating losses and represents a drag on the group's overall profitability;
  • the arrival of new contracts of exceptional size, such as orders related to the Small Modular Reactors (SMR) projects or large data center infrastructure investments, which could substantially increase the backlog already above 120 billion USD and bring further visibility on revenues and margins after 2028.

In a scenario where these factors materialize, I believe a much higher target price is plausible, consistent with valuations above USD 500 per share, thanks to:

  • Potential fair value EV = 4.6 bn EBITDA × 30 (highest multiple) = 138 bn USD
  • Potential equity fair value = 138 + 8 = 146 bn USD
  • Potential target price = 146 bn USD / 273 million shares = 535 USD/share

The 30x EV/EBITDA multiple is my guess, based on GEV's current valuation and strong growth scenarios, as has historically occurred in similar companies.

However, in the absence of these developments, I believe that the current valuation already incorporates most of the growth expectations. The analysis of the official documents available in the GE Vernova Investor Relations section confirms how the challenges in the Wind segment and the margins still lower than competitors make operational execution a decisive factor for the sustainability of such high multiples.

Personally, I consider GE Vernova an interesting long-term opportunity for those who want to expose themselves to the electrification and decarbonization megatrends, but I believe that at current prices the risk/return profile is unbalanced: I see new entries justified only in the event of a correction that brings the stock back to multiples more in line with the sector fundamentals or if the company manages to surprise the market with above-expected results.

However, in the absence of these developments, I believe that the current valuation already incorporates most of the growth expectations. Personally, I consider GE Vernova an interesting long-term opportunity for those who want to expose themselves to the electrification and decarbonization megatrends, but the current prices would seem right. I see new entries justified only in the event of a correction that brings the stock back to multiples more in line with the sector fundamentals, or if the company manages to surprise the market with above-expected results.

Risks

Despite the excellent long-term prospects, I see several risk factors that could weigh on GE Vernova's performance and the sustainability of its current multiples:

  1. Margins still volatile in Wind. The Wind segment continues to record significant operating losses (EBITDA margin still negative at -7.9% in 1Q'25). Without a structural improvement, wind risks remaining a drag on overall profitability
  2. Dependence on the industrial investment cycle and data centers. A large part of the growth expected by GEV is based on demand for new power plants, network infrastructure and hyperscale data centers. A slowdown in investments in these sectors could reduce new orders or lead to postponements, eroding visibility on revenues.
  3. Geopolitical and regulatory risks. GE Vernova has significant exposure to international markets and projects subject to permits and regulations (e.g. nuclear, large grid infrastructure). Regulatory changes, trade tensions (such as US-China tariffs) or political instability in key areas could delay projects or increase costs.
  4. Tight Valuation. The stock trades at multiples well above the industry average. This means that even small disappointments on margins, backlog or future guidance could trigger significant corrections, increasing volatility for shareholders.

Opportunities

Despite the risks, I see several factors that could be significant catalysts for GE Vernova in the coming years:

  1. Structural demand for power capacity: the global energy transition and electrification of the economy require huge investments in new power plants, high-efficiency turbines and more robust grids. GEV is positioned at the forefront.
  2. Rising gas turbine pricing: average gas turbine prices have more than doubled in recent years (from around $1,200–$1,400/kW to ~$2,400/kW, source: Bloomberg Intelligence). If this momentum continues, as the current level of demand suggests, GEV could benefit from further margin expansion in the Power and Electrification segments.
  3. Small Modular Reactor Development: GE Vernova is among the companies best positioned to benefit from the growing interest in modular nuclear reactors. The potential acquisition of SMR contracts could open a new source of multi-year revenues starting in 2029, further expanding the backlog and diversifying the portfolio.
  4. Maintenance: gas turbines tend to generate a strong increase in service revenues when they enter the maintenance phase, typically 3–4 years after delivery

My take

I believe that GE Vernova is one of the best positioned companies to take advantage of the growing global demand for electricity and the energy transition, thanks to a record backlog and progressive improvement in margins. For me, it is a stock to keep in the portfolio to take advantage of all the possible catalysts listed above. On the other hand, I believe that at current prices, much of this prospect is already priced into the stock: the valuation already incorporates very aggressive expectations on improving profitability, especially in the Wind segment.

For this reason, I consider the stock attractive for the long term, but I think it is more prudent to wait for a possible correction or concrete signs of improvement in margins before entering. In a scenario of margins above current guidance and significant new orders, I still see room for a target price well above $500 per share.

In light of these factors, my rating is Hold, waiting for operational confirmations or a more interesting entry point.
